text: The defining moment Zimbabwean constitutional reform came 2008 after the disputed and violence riddled elections 2008 when the three main political parties entered into transitional Government National Unity and were tasked with the establishment new constitution which was ultimately adopted 2013 following protracted and turbulent process which began 2009. Some segments the civil society however argued that the concerned political parties had 'captured the constitutional project and narrowed short-term struggle motivated the pursuit party political interests the expense the will the people and nation's broad long-term interests', and thereby subverted and/or negated the aspirations the people. against this background that the study therefore assesses the participation, role and significance the rural populace the drafting the 2013 Zimbabwean Constitution. The study traces the history constitutional reform efforts Zimbabwe, beginning with the colonial Lancaster House Constitution 1979, the protracted exercise 2009 2013 which gave birth the current Constitution. focuses the 2009-13 constitution making process case study. The study employs approach adopting both doctrinal and empirical research approaches. The study employed the doctrinal research approach provide for doctrinal analysis the relevant global, regional and domestic legislation and case law. The empirical research approach, through interviews, was used collect qualitative data from the general members the rural populace and key institutions such political parties and human rights organisations from three selected rural districts, namely Bulilima, Makonde and Mutasa. The study indicated that while significant number the rural populace participated the constitution making process, the legal environment which subsisted during the constitution making process did not allow for the unfettered flow information and ideas, direct result repressive legislation such AIPPA, Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act, Interception Communications Act and POSA, among others similar laws, and well the deeply polarised political environment owing the nature the relationship between the ZANU PF-led government and the opposition political parties. The study further showed that the process was heavily dominated the political parties the Global Political Agreement and all the political parties wanted ensure the adoption constitution that best reflected their preferences and partisan views rather than the will the masses, making the 2013 Constitution elitist negotiated document, contrary the provisions Article the GPA which provided for the right Zimbabweans make constitution for themselves and themselves.

The 2018 Berlin anti-semitic attack was attack street the German capital Berlin. Two young men wearing Jewish skullcaps were insulted Arabic-speaking passers-by. One the two, Israeli citizen, was beaten with belt Palestinian attacker. Video footage led public outrage.
There have been other similar incidents involving Muslim assailants particular. spokesman for the Jewish community said the attack showed that "Jewish people are not safe here".
The number overall anti-Semitic offenses Berlin has been rising recent years. 2017, police had registered 288 anti-Semitic acts; 2016, 197 anti-Semitic cases had been recorded. The Research and Information Center Antisemitism Berlin (RIAS) counts total 947 anti-Semitic incidents for 2017, but there may unrecorded cases.
April 2018, Adam Armoush, 21-year-old Israeli Arab living Berlin and his German friend Moroccan origin went through street Prenzlauer Berg taking selfies while wearing kippahs. According Armoush, was trying win argument with Jewish friends his Israel proving that could walk through Berlin wearing Jewish skullcap. one point, argument with three Arab-speaking young men started, which was followed violent attack. Armoush managed document the violence with his cell phone and later allowed the video shared Facebook, from where the video went viral. The video shows the main attacker, later identified 19-year-old Palestinian from refugee camp Syria who had come Germany war refugee, beat Armoush repeatedly with his belt. During the onslaught, the attacker shouted the word "Yahudi", the Arabic word for Jew, before one his companions intervened and dragged him away. According Armoush, the main attacker had also tried hurt him with glass bottle. consequence the beating, Armoush suffered minor injuries. Before turning himself police, the main attacker contacted representative the Palestinian student community Berlin and appeared short video that was distributed social media. They denied was hostile towards Jews that his actions had been motivated anti-Semitism.
The main attacker turned himself accompanied his lawyer the day after the incident. arrest warrant against him was executed.
Several politicians, among them chancellor Angela Merkel and foreign minister Heiko Maas condemned the attack. the Israeli television, Merkel warned persistent hatred Jews Germany. She spoke "different form anti-Semitism" coming from people Arab descent. After this and other incidents, the President the Central Council Jews Germany, Josef Schuster, warned Jewish men "not show themselves openly with kippah the metropolitan milieu Germany".
Under the slogan "Berlin Wears Kippah" the Jewish community Berlin called all citizens wear the kippah April solidarity with the victims.
"Anti-Semitic crimes increase dramatically Germany". Jewish Telegraphic Agency. August 2018. Retrieved August 2018.
Eddy, Melissa; Curry, Andrew (18 April 2018). "Fury Germany Rap Duo With Anti-Jewish Lyrics Gets Award". New York Times. Retrieved April 2018.
"'Jewish Man' Attacked Berlin Admits He's Israeli-Arab Who Didn't Believe Germany Was anti-Semitic". Haaretz. DPA, AP. April 2018.
